book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
Cruelty of Macedonius, and Tumults raised by him.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: Cruelty of Macedonius, and Tumults raised by him.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The bishops of the Arian party began to assume greater assurance from the imperial edicts.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: In what manner they undertook to convene a Synod, we will explain somewhat later.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Let us now briefly mention a few of their previous acts.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Acacius and Patrophilus having ejected Maximus, bishop of Jerusalem, installed Cyril in his see.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Macedonius subverted the order of things in the cities and provinces adjacent to Constantinople, promoting to ecclesiastical honors his assistants in his intrigues against the churches.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He ordained Eleusius bishop of Cyzicus, and Marathonius, bishop of Nicomedia: the latter had before been a deacon under Macedonius himself, and proved very active in founding monasteries both of men and women.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But we must now mention in what way Macedonius desolated the churches in the cities and provinces around Constantinople.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This man, as I have already said, having seized the bishopric, inflicted innumerable calamities on such as were unwilling to adopt his views.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: His persecutions were not confined to those who were recognized as members of the catholic church, but extended to the Novatians also, inasmuch as he knew that they maintained the doctrine of the homoousion; they therefore with the others underwent the most intolerable sufferings, but their bishop, Angelius by name, effected his escape by flight.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Many persons eminent for their piety were seized and tortured, because they refused to communicate with him: and after the torture, they forcibly constrained the men to be partakers of the holy mysteries, their mouths being forced open with a piece of wood, and then the consecrated elements thrust into them.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Those who were so treated regarded this as a punishment far more grievous than all others.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Moreover they laid hold of women and children, and compelled them to be initiated [by baptism]; and if any one resisted or otherwise spoke against it, stripes immediately followed, and after the stripes, bonds and imprisonment, and other violent measures.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: I shall here relate an instance or two whereby the reader may form some idea of the extent of the harshness and cruelty exercised by Macedonius and those who were then in power.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: They first pressed in a box, and then sawed off, the breasts of such women as were unwilling to communicate with them.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The same parts of the persons. of other women they burnt partly with iron, and partly with eggs intensely heated in the fire.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This mode of torture which was unknown even among the heathen, was invented by those who professed to be Christians.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: These facts were related to me by the aged Auxanon, the presbyter in the Novatian church of whom I spoke in the first book.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He said also that he had himself endured not a few severities from the Arians, prior to his reaching the dignity of presbyter; having been thrown into prison and beaten with many stripes, together with Alexander the Paphlagonian, his companion in the monastic life.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He added that he had himself been able to sustain these tortures, but that Alexander died in prison from the effects of their infliction.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He is now buried on the right of those sailing into the bay of Constantinople which is called Ceras, close by the rivers, where there is a church of the Novatians named after Alexander.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Moreover the Arians, at the instigation of Macedonius, demolished with many other churches in various cities, that of the Novatians at Constantinople near Pelargus.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Why I particularly mention this church, will be seen from the extraordinary circumstances connected with it, as testified by the same aged Auxanon.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The emperor’s edict and the violence of Macedonius had doomed to destruction the churches of those who maintained the doctrine of consubstantiality; the decree and violence reached this church, and those also who were charged with the execution of the mandate were at hand to carry it into effect.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: I cannot but admire the zeal displayed by the Novatians on this occasion, as well as the sympathy they experienced from those whom the Arians at that time ejected, but who are now in peaceful possession of their churches.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For when the emissaries of their enemies were urgent to accomplish its destruction, an immense multitude of Novatians, aided by numbers of others who held similar sentiments, having assembled around this devoted church, pulled it down, and conveyed the materials of it to another place: this place stands opposite the city, and is called Sycæ, and forms the thirteenth ward of the town of Constantinople.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This removal was effected in a very short time, from the extraordinary ardor of the numerous persons engaged in it: one carried tiles, another stones, a third timber; some loading themselves with one thing, and some with another.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Even women and children assisted in the work, regarding it as the realization of their best wishes, and esteeming it the greatest honor to be accounted the faithful guardians of things consecrated to God.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: In this way at that time was the church of the Novatians transported to Sycæ.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Long afterwards when Constantius was dead, the emperor Julian ordered its former site to be restored, and permitted them to rebuild it there.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The people therefore, as before, having carried back the materials, reared the church in its former position; and from this circumstance, and its great improvement in structure and ornament, they not inappropriately called it Anastasia.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The church as we before said was restored afterwards in the reign of Julian.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But at that time both the Catholics and the Novatians were alike subjected to persecution: for the former abominated offering their devotions in those churches in which the Arians assembled, but frequented the other three —for this is the number of the churches which the Novatians have in the city—and engaged in divine service with them.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Indeed they would have been wholly united, had not the Novatians refused from regard to their ancient precepts.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: In other respects however, they mutually maintained such a degree of cordiality and affection, as to be ready to lay down their lives for one another: both parties were therefore persecuted indiscriminately, not only at Constantinople, but also in other provinces and cities.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: At Cyzicus, Eleusius, the bishop of that place, perpetrated the same kind of enormities against the Christians there, as Macedonius had done elsewhere, harassing and putting them to flight in all directions and [among other things] he completely demolished the church of the Novatians at Cyzicus.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But Macedonius consummated his wickedness in the following manner.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Hearing that there was a great number of the Novatian sect in the province of Paphlagonia, and especially at Mantinium, and perceiving that such a numerous body could not be driven from their homes by ecclesiastics alone, he caused, by the emperor’s permission, four companies of soldiers to be sent into Paphlagonia, that through dread of the military they might receive the Arian opinion.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But those who inhabited Mantinium, animated to desperation by zeal for their religion, armed themselves with long reap-hooks, hatchets, and whatever weapon came to hand, and went forth to meet the troops; on which a conflict ensuing, many indeed of the Paphlagonians were slain, but nearly all the soldiers were destroyed.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: I learnt these things from a Paphlagonian peasant who said that he was present at the engagement; and many others of that province corroborate this account.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Such were the exploits of Macedonius on behalf of Christianity, consisting of murders, battles, incarcerations, and civil wars: proceedings which rendered him odious not only to the objects of his persecution, but even to his own party.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He became obnoxious also to the emperor on these accounts, and particularly so from the circumstance I am about to relate.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The church where the coffin lay that contained the relics of the emperor Constantine threatened to fall.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: On this account those that entered, as well as those who were accustomed to remain there for devotional purposes, were in much fear.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Macedonius, therefore, wished to remove the emperor’s remains, lest the coffin should be injured by the ruins.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The populace getting intelligence of this, endeavored to prevent it, insisting ‘that the emperor’s bones should not be disturbed, as such a disinterment would be equivalent, to their being dug up’: many however affirmed that its removal could not possibly injure the dead body, and thus two parties were formed on this question; such as held the doctrine of consubstantiality joining with those who opposed it on the ground of its impiety.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Macedonius, in total disregard of these prejudices, caused the emperor’s remains to be transported to the church where those of the martyr Acacius lay.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Whereupon a vast multitude rushed toward that edifice in two hostile divisions, which attacked one another with great fury, and great loss of life was occasioned, so that the churchyard was covered with gore, and the well also which was in it overflowed with blood, which ran into the adjacent portico, and thence even into the very street.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: When the emperor was informed of this unfortunate occurrence, he was highly incensed against Macedonius, both on account of the slaughter which he had occasioned, and because he had dared to move his father’s body without consulting him.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Having therefore left the Cæsar Julian to take care of the western parts, he himself set out for the east.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: How Macedonius was a short time afterwards deposed, and thus suffered a most inadequate punishment for his infamous crimes, I shall hereafter relate.
